scenes.text: This is Sift, a two-persona database reliability product. Real developers generate live MongoDB workload, and one D B R E triages it with A I that only ever recommends. I'm Dev. Let me sign in and show you.

From my workload console I run a real, read-only query against our live Atlas collection. And it's slow. A blocking, in-memory sort: fifty thousand documents scanned just to return twenty. Sift captures that explain evidence and attributes it to me, automatically.
I'm Aakash, another developer. I run my own workload. Same story, captured and attributed to me. Real queries, real execution evidence, real people behind every one.

Now I'm the D B R E. I don't guess, and I don't stare at dashboards. I see every slow query my users actually ran, ranked by real explain evidence, and each one is attributed to the person who ran it.

I pick the worst offender and diagnose it. Three Gemini Agent Engine roles, Diagnose, Candidate, and Rationale, run four read-only tools to explain the plan, compare candidate indexes, and reason about the fix. Every one of them reads. None of them can write.
Because the agents only advise. Deterministic Python is the decision-maker. It selects the index by the Equality, Sort, Range rule, and validates that the agent's proposal matches, exactly.

Then it computes an evidence hash and binds my approval to it. Nothing touches the database until I approve this precise hash. The apply, and the re-explain that verifies it, are backend-only. The agents never get near a mutation.

And Sift Memory adds Voyage-powered retrieval. Read-only operator context, just for me, never a decision-maker. Real users, real evidence, A I that recommends, deterministic Python that decides, and a human who approves. That's Sift.
